title: Authority Over Disease, Nature, and Demons
objective: See how Jesus' miracles in Matthew 8-9 validate his teaching by demonstrating divine authority over sickness, the natural world, and spiritual evil

required_passages.text: When he came down from the mountain, great crowds followed him. And behold, a leper came to him and knelt before him, saying, 'Lord, if you will, you can make me clean.' And Jesus stretched out his hand and touched him, saying, 'I will; be clean.' And immediately his leprosy was cleansed. Then a centurion came forward to him, appealing to him, 'Lord, my servant is lying paralyzed at home, suffering terribly.' And he said to him, 'I will come and heal him.' But the centurion replied, 'Lord, I am not worthy to have you come under my roof, but only say the word, and my servant will be healed. For I too am a man under authority, with soldiers under me. And I say to one, Go, and he goes, and to another, Come, and he comes, and to my servant, Do this, and he does it.' When Jesus heard this, he marveled and said to those who followed him, 'Truly, I tell you, with no one in Israel have I found such faith.' And to the centurion Jesus said, 'Go; let it be done for you as you have believed.' And the servant was healed at that very moment. And when Jesus entered Peter's house, he saw his mother-in-law lying sick with a fever. He touched her hand, and the fever left her, and she rose and began to serve him. That evening they brought to him many who were oppressed by demons, and he cast out the spirits with a word and healed all who were sick. This was to fulfill what was spoken by the prophet Isaiah: 'He took our illnesses and bore our diseases.' And when he got into the boat, his disciples followed him. And behold, there arose a great storm on the sea, so that the boat was being swamped by the waves; but he was asleep. And they went and woke him, saying, 'Save us, Lord; we are perishing.' And he said to them, 'Why are you afraid, O you of little faith?' Then he rose and rebuked the winds and the sea, and there was a great calm. And the men marveled, saying, 'What sort of man is this, that even winds and sea obey him?' And when he came to the other side, to the country of the Gadarenes, two demon-possessed men met him, coming out of the tombs, so fierce that no one could pass that way. And behold, they cried out, 'What have you to do with us, O Son of God? Have you come here to torment us before the time?' Now a herd of many pigs was feeding at some distance from them. And the demons begged him, saying, 'If you cast us out, send us away into the herd of pigs.' And he said to them, 'Go.' So they came out and went into the pigs, and behold, the whole herd rushed down the steep bank into the sea and drowned in the waters.

segments.audio_script: Welcome to lesson ten. The Sermon on the Mount ended with the crowds astonished because Jesus taught as one who had authority. Matthew 8 and 9 now show what that authority looks like in action. Jesus comes down from the mountain and immediately begins healing the sick, calming a violent storm, and delivering men from demonic possession. Today we examine a selection of these miracles and what they reveal about who Jesus is.

Matthew chapter 8, verses 1 through 34. When he came down from the mountain, great crowds followed him. And behold, a leper came to him and knelt before him, saying, Lord, if you will, you can make me clean. And Jesus stretched out his hand and touched him, saying, I will; be clean. And immediately his leprosy was cleansed. Then a centurion came forward to him, appealing to him, Lord, my servant is lying paralyzed at home, suffering terribly. But the centurion replied, Lord, I am not worthy to have you come under my roof, but only say the word, and my servant will be healed. For I too am a man under authority, with soldiers under me. When Jesus heard this, he marveled and said, Truly, I tell you, with no one in Israel have I found such faith. And the servant was healed at that very moment. And when Jesus entered Peter's house, he saw his mother-in-law lying sick with a fever. He touched her hand, and the fever left her, and she rose and began to serve him. That evening they brought to him many who were oppressed by demons, and he cast out the spirits with a word and healed all who were sick. This was to fulfill what was spoken by the prophet Isaiah: He took our illnesses and bore our diseases. And when he got into the boat, his disciples followed him. And behold, there arose a great storm on the sea, so that the boat was being swamped by the waves; but he was asleep. And they went and woke him, saying, Save us, Lord; we are perishing. And he said to them, Why are you afraid, O you of little faith? Then he rose and rebuked the winds and the sea, and there was a great calm. And the men marveled, saying, What sort of man is this, that even winds and sea obey him? And when he came to the other side, two demon-possessed men met him, coming out of the tombs, so fierce that no one could pass that way. And they cried out, What have you to do with us, O Son of God? Have you come here to torment us before the time? And the demons begged him, saying, If you cast us out, send us away into the herd of pigs. And he said to them, Go. So they came out and went into the pigs, and behold, the whole herd rushed down the steep bank into the sea and drowned.

Matthew presents ten miracles across chapters 8 and 9, deliberately grouping them to make a cumulative theological argument. The number ten echoes the ten plagues of Egypt, where God demonstrated his supremacy over every domain of Pharaoh's world. Here, Jesus demonstrates supremacy over every domain of human suffering. The first miracle involves a leper. Leprosy placed a person outside the community by law — untouchable, excluded, declared unclean. Jesus touches him. It is the first and most dramatic gesture of the entire miracle section: the holy does not become defiled by contact with the unclean. Instead, cleansing flows outward from Jesus into the unclean.

Three miracles are worth examining closely. First, the centurion. He is a Gentile military officer — doubly outside the expected circle of recipients — yet Jesus marvels at his faith and says he has found nothing like it in all Israel. The centurion understands authority: he knows that Jesus only needs to speak and it will be done. His model of faith is built on understanding who Jesus is, not on physical proximity to him. Second, the calming of the storm. The disciples wake Jesus in panic. His first response is to question their faith rather than immediately calm the water. The storm is not only the occasion for a miracle; it is a test of whether they trust who is in the boat with them. Third, the Gadarene demons. They recognise Jesus as the Son of God and know their time of authority is limited. Even the demonic realm acknowledges his identity, making the crowd's reaction — asking Jesus to leave the area — tragically ironic.

Three themes emerge from these miracles. The first is the scope of Jesus' authority. He commands disease, weather, and demonic powers with equal ease. Nothing in the created or fallen order lies outside his jurisdiction. The second theme is the unexpected location of faith. A leper, a Gentile soldier, fishermen terrified in a storm — these are the people who encounter and respond to Jesus in this chapter. The religiously credentialed are largely absent. The third theme is prophetic fulfillment. Matthew explicitly links Jesus' healing ministry to Isaiah 53:4, he took our illnesses and bore our diseases. The miracles are not merely compassion; they are signs of the servant who is bearing humanity's brokenness as part of his mission.

Review question: What did the crowds ask after Jesus calmed the storm, and what does the question reveal? Answer: They asked, What sort of man is this, that even winds and sea obey him? The question reveals that the disciples had not yet understood his full identity — they were still working out who Jesus was.
segments.correct_answer: What sort of man is this, that even winds and sea obey him — showing they did not yet understand his identity
acceptable_variants: what kind of man is this that even wind and sea obey him
they marveled asking what manner of man this is
the disciples were astonished and questioned who jesus really was
they asked who he is that even nature obeys him

segments.audio_script: You have completed lesson ten. Matthew has shown that Jesus' authority is not limited to words on a hillside. It extends over the body, over creation itself, and over spiritual evil. In lesson eleven we enter Matthew 13, where Jesus will begin teaching the mysteries of the kingdom through a series of parables.

vocabulary.definition: A person afflicted with a skin disease classified as leprosy under Mosaic law; such individuals were legally excluded from the community, required to announce their condition publicly, and were considered ritually unclean
A sharp command of authority; when Jesus rebukes the wind and the sea, he addresses them as a superior commanding an inferior, revealing his authority over the natural order
